output_text:
Reviewed the section as an intro/method-overview blurb and identified specific overclaims, a logical gap, and underspecified details — followed by a tightened rewrite.

## Critique and concrete recommendations

**1. The distribution-independence argument is a non-sequitur (most important issue).**
> "Because T_phi is a function of the model's internal representations rather than of input statistics, the calibration map transfers to arbitrary downstream test distributions without recalibration."

Internal representations are deterministic functions of inputs, so their distribution shifts when the input distribution shifts. The conclusion doesn't follow from the premise. Either (a) drop the causal claim and present transfer as an empirical finding, or (b) replace it with a real argument — e.g., that attention-entropy statistics shift less than logit statistics under the shifts you test, ideally backed by a measurement (e.g., KL between source/target entropy distributions vs. source/target logit distributions).

**2. "Arbitrary deployment distributions" is overclaimed.**
ImageNet-C (synthetic corruptions of the same classes) and ImageNet-Sketch (style shift, same label space) are two specific shift types. They are not "arbitrary." Recommend: "a range of distribution shifts including synthetic corruptions and style shift," and remove "arbitrary" from the opening sentence. The safety-critical framing should also be softened or cut — the evidence base doesn't support it.

**3. The 35–45% ECE reduction needs context.**
- Relative to *vanilla* temperature scaling only — readers will ask about Dirichlet calibration, ensemble temperature scaling, Mahalanobis-style confidence, and trainable-bin methods. Either include them as baselines or explicitly defer.
- Report absolute ECE alongside the relative number. A 40% reduction from 0.04 to 0.024 reads differently than 0.12 to 0.072.
- "Across all settings" is ambiguous — does it hold at ImageNet-C severity 5? Per-setting numbers belong in a table; the prose should say "on every (severity, corruption) combination we evaluated" or "on average across severities," whichever is true.

**4. Training/evaluation split on ImageNet-val is underspecified.**
You train T_phi on "a labeled subset of ImageNet-val" and then evaluate on "the standard ImageNet-val set." State the split explicitly (sizes, disjointness, seeds) — otherwise readers will assume contamination.

**5. NLL training vs. ECE reporting.**
Standard but worth a sentence acknowledging the objective/metric mismatch, or showing Brier score / NLL on the test sets too.

**6. The [CLS]-entropy/correctness correlation is asserted, not shown.**
"Correlates strongly" needs either a number (e.g., point-biserial / AUROC for correctness prediction from entropy alone) or a forward reference to a figure. Currently it reads as motivation by assertion.

**7. T_phi specification gaps.**
- Per-sample scalar, or per-class? "Scalar temperature for the final logits" suggests one scalar per sample — say so.
- Input dimension = number of blocks. Make that explicit (12 for ViT-B, 24 for ViT-L, etc.) and say how T_phi generalizes across depths if you evaluate multiple backbones.
- MLP width/depth/activation belong in the methods proper, not here, but at least the input/output shapes should be unambiguous in the overview.

**8. Section 5 forward reference is stated as a fact.**
"The same framework produces calibrated zero-shot classification confidences across 12 benchmarks" — fine in an intro, but use "we show that" rather than presenting it as already-established here. As written it blurs claim with evidence.

**9. Last paragraph generalizes from a forward reference.**
"Supporting our claim that representation-based calibration is broadly transferable" — leans on Section 5 results to support a claim the current section already made. Pick one place to make the transferability argument and have the evidence live next to it.

## Tightened rewrite (conservative claims only)

> We propose **ConfViT**, a post-hoc calibration framework for vision transformers. Unlike prior post-hoc calibrators that operate on output logits, ConfViT derives its calibration signal from the model's intermediate attention representations — specifically, the per-block entropy of the [CLS] token's attention distribution, which we show (§3.2) is predictive of prediction correctness.
>
> ConfViT learns a calibration map T_φ that takes the per-block [CLS]-entropy vector and outputs a per-sample temperature applied to the final logits. T_φ is a small MLP trained by minimizing NLL on a held-out split of ImageNet-val (disjoint from the evaluation split; details in §3.3). At inference, T_φ rescales the softmax in a single forward pass.
>
> We evaluate on (i) ImageNet-val, (ii) ImageNet-C across severities 1–5, and (iii) ImageNet-Sketch. Without any retuning on the shifted distributions, ConfViT reduces ECE relative to vanilla temperature scaling by 35–45% (absolute ECE in Table 2) and also outperforms [stronger baselines] on every setting we evaluated. We attribute this transfer to the empirical observation that [CLS]-entropy statistics shift less under these distribution shifts than logit statistics do (§4.3); we do not claim transfer to arbitrary unseen shifts.
>
> In §5 we extend ConfViT to CLIP-style dual encoders and evaluate calibrated zero-shot confidences on 12 downstream classification benchmarks.

## Uncertainty disclosure
- The "non-sequitur" critique (item 1) is based only on this passage; if §4 actually proves a representation-invariance property under specific shift assumptions, the prose just needs to point to it. I haven't seen the rest of the paper.
- Recommendations for additional baselines (Dirichlet, ensemble TS, Mahalanobis) assume your venue's reviewers will expect them; check recent calibration papers in your target venue to confirm.
- The framing of the rewrite assumes you have absolute ECE numbers and an entropy-vs-logit shift comparison to point to. If you don't, those sentences need to change.
